content:
*The pew wood gives a small groan as I lean in, the Psalm floating over us like a breeze through tall grass. My boy’s knee is swinging, heel ticking the hymnal rack, eyes trying to track the reader’s voice.*

I tap the bulletin where they’ve printed the text and whisper, just loud enough for him alone. "Listen for the part where it says the sky talks."

*The line comes — the sky proclaims — and I see his mouth shape the word before it lands in his ears. I don’t add a lecture. I let the room carry it. My hand stays on the page, steady, so he has something to anchor to while the words run their course like the sun it’s about to name.*

When the reader hits "a tent for the sun," I nudge him once and point, not up — that would be theater — but to the margin where I’ve drawn a small circle and an arrow from east to west. He grins like he’s been let in on a secret that’s not actually a secret.

After the Amen, I don’t pick a fight in the aisle. I shake the elder’s hand, same as always, and mean it. Unity is a good word if you don’t use it to sand the truth down. There’s a time for the table and a calm question; this moment belongs to my son.

So I take him outside. *The noon light is blunt and warm; the air smells like dust and cut grass.* I hold my palm flat at the eastern edge of the sky and sweep it across to the west. "What did the Psalm say the sun does?"

"Runs," he says. "Like a happy runner."

"That’s right." I keep my voice plain. "Poetry’s the way we talk when the real thing is too big to say straight. It doesn’t make it less true; it makes it land. That’s the sky. That’s the sun God set to run it. You can watch it if you’re patient."

We stand there for the kind of minute a ten-year-old can bear. A swallow cuts a dark stitch high over the roofline. I don’t push past the hold he has. I just give him one more line to carry. "Next time you hear someone say it’s ‘just poetry,’ try this in your head: it’s poetry, and it’s true. Both."

Later in the week, I ask the elder for a quiet talk. "Help me, brother," I say, not sharp. "My boy heard Psalm 19 on Sunday. I want him to know the words he hears in here still mean what they name out there. If we call the beginning ‘just’ poetry for the sake of peace, what are we teaching him when he looks up?" Then I listen. I can carry unity and truth at the same time if I keep my hands open and my feet on the ground.

But next Sunday? In the pew, with my ten-year-old? I point to the word sky when it’s read. I take him outside to stand under it. I let the day preach the rest.
